items.q: Why do sociologists use official statistics on inequality cautiously?
items.correct: Because they can be socially constructed and may hide or distort real differences.
items.misconception: Official statistics are always perfectly objective.
items.random_wrong: Statistics cannot measure inequality at all.
items.why: How data is defined and collected shapes the figures, so interpretivists treat them critically.

items.q: Is social class the only form of inequality sociologists study?
items.correct: No - they also study gender, ethnicity, age and disability.
items.random_wrong: Inequality no longer exists in modern society.
items.why: Inequality is intersectional; class interacts with gender, ethnicity and other divisions.

items.q: What does the Goldthorpe scheme classify people by?
items.correct: Their occupation and employment relations (their market and work situation).
items.why: Goldthorpe (and the later NS-SEC) groups people by employment relations, not just earnings.

items.q: Goldthorpe's scheme is the basis of which UK classification?
items.why: It's NS-SEC. “RG” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The concept of an 'underclass' in 1990 came from:
items.why: It's Murray. “Wright” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many classes are in Goldthorpe's scheme?
items.why: Exact answer: 7 classes. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
